title: Jambalaya
subtitle: Creole one-pot rice with chicken, andouille sausage, and the holy trinity
description: Creole jambalaya is a Louisiana one-pot: chicken and andouille sausage browned in the same pan, the holy trinity of onion, celery, and green pepper cooked in the drippings, then rice added and cooked directly in seasoned stock until every grain has absorbed the flavour. It is a party dish in every sense — easy to scale, economical, intensely satisfying, and deeply coloured from the paprika and sausage fat. Everything cooks in a single heavy pan.
sourceNotes: Creole jambalaya is the New Orleans variation of the Cajun original, distinguished by the addition of tomatoes. The dish descends from Spanish paella and West African rice-cooking traditions, filtered through Louisiana Creole culture. The recipe appears in The Picayune's Creole Cook Book (1901), the foundational reference for New Orleans Creole cooking.

recipeTools.notes: A wide, heavy-based pan with a tight-fitting lid — 28–30 cm. The wide base ensures the rice cooks evenly and doesn't pile up in the centre.

glossaryTerms.definition: The aromatic base of Cajun and Creole cooking: onion, celery, and green (bell) pepper. The Louisiana equivalent of the French mirepoix or Italian soffritto.

ingredients.notes: Sliced into 1 cm rounds — or smoked sausage

steps: Heat the oil in a large, wide, heavy casserole over a high heat. Brown the sausage slices for 3–4 minutes until the fat has rendered and the cut faces are well coloured. Remove with a slotted spoon.
In the same fat, brown the chicken pieces for 4–5 minutes. Remove and set aside with the sausage.

Reduce to medium heat. Add the onion, celery, and green pepper to the pan. Cook for 5–6 minutes until softened. Add the garlic and all the dried spices — paprika, thyme, oregano, cayenne, salt, pepper — and cook for 1 minute.
Add the tinned tomatoes and cook for 2 minutes, scraping up any fond.
Return the chicken and sausage to the pan. Add the rice and stir to coat in the spiced vegetable mixture. Pour in the hot chicken stock and add the bay leaves.
Bring to a simmer, stir once, then cover tightly and reduce the heat to its lowest. Cook for 20–25 minutes until the rice has absorbed all the liquid.
Remove from the heat. Leave covered for 5 minutes, then remove the bay leaves. Fluff gently with a fork.
Scatter with spring onions and parsley. Serve from the pan.

content.text: (onion, celery, and green pepper) is the flavour foundation. Once the rice and stock go in, resist the urge to stir; disturbing the rice during cooking releases starch and makes it gluey. Stir once when the stock goes in, then leave it alone.

Heat {{olive-oil}} of oil in a large casserole over a high heat. Brown {{andouille-sausage}} of sausage slices for 3–4 minutes until coloured. Remove. In the same fat, brown {{chicken-thigh-boneless-skinless}} of chicken for 4–5 minutes. Remove and set aside with the sausage.
Cook the trinity and build the rice

Reduce to medium heat. Cook the onion, celery, and green pepper for 5–6 minutes. Add the garlic and all the dried spices for 1 minute. Add the tinned tomatoes and cook for 2 minutes. Return the chicken and sausage, add {{long-grain-rice}} of rice, and stir to coat. Pour in {{chicken-stock}} of hot stock and add the bay leaves. Bring to a simmer, stir once, cover tightly, and reduce to the lowest heat. Cook for 20–25 minutes until the liquid is absorbed. Rest covered for 5 minutes, remove the bay leaves, fluff with a fork, and serve scattered with spring onions and parsley.
